meta.title: C# / .NET
meta.description: Review checklist for C# and .NET applications

items.text: Is async/await used consistently without mixing .Result or .Wait()?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Calling .Result or .Wait() on a Task in an async context can deadlock, especially in ASP.NET. Async should propagate all the way up the call stack.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Mixing sync and async causes thread pool starvation and deadlocks. The classic ASP.NET deadlock occurs when .Result blocks the synchronization context that the awaited task needs to resume on.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for .Result and .Wait() on Task objects. Ensure the calling method is async and uses await instead.
baseContent.exampleComment: This .Result call will deadlock in the ASP.NET request pipeline. Change the method to async and await the task instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never call .Result or .Wait() in async code. Async all the way.

items.text: Is ConfigureAwait(false) used in library code where appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: ConfigureAwait(false) tells the awaiter not to capture the synchronization context. Library code should use it to avoid deadlocks and improve performance.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without ConfigureAwait(false), library code captures the caller's SynchronizationContext, which can cause deadlocks when the caller blocks. It also wastes resources posting back to the original context.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check await calls in library code (not ASP.NET controllers). They should use ConfigureAwait(false). Application-level code in ASP.NET Core generally does not need it.
baseContent.exampleComment: This library method awaits without ConfigureAwait(false). Add it to prevent deadlocks when called from UI or legacy ASP.NET contexts.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Library code: ConfigureAwait(false). App code in ASP.NET Core: not needed.

items.text: Are CancellationTokens threaded through async operations?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Long-running async operations should accept a CancellationToken so callers (like HTTP request pipelines) can cancel them when the client disconnects.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without cancellation support, abandoned requests continue consuming resources. In ASP.NET, this means wasted database queries and CPU after the client has already disconnected.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check async methods that do I/O or long computation. Do they accept and pass through a CancellationToken?
baseContent.exampleComment: This database query does not accept a CancellationToken. Pass the request's cancellation token to stop the query if the client disconnects.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Thread CancellationTokens through every async chain. Let callers cancel cleanly.

items.text: Are fire-and-forget tasks avoided or properly handled?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Calling an async method without awaiting it means exceptions are silently swallowed and the operation may not complete before the process exits.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unobserved task exceptions can crash the process (depending on framework version) or silently lose data. Fire-and-forget tasks are a common source of hard-to-debug production issues.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for async method calls without await. If fire-and-forget is intentional, ensure the task is at least logged on failure.
baseContent.exampleComment: SendEmailAsync is called without await. If it fails, the error is silently lost. Either await it or use a background queue.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always await async calls. Use a background job system for true fire-and-forget work.

items.text: Are IEnumerable results materialized before multiple enumeration?

baseContent.whatItMeans: IEnumerable is lazily evaluated. Enumerating it multiple times re-executes the query (including database calls). Call .ToList() or .ToArray() to materialize.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Double enumeration of a database query means two round trips. Even for in-memory queries, it doubles the computation. Worse, results may differ between enumerations if the source changes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if an IEnumerable variable is used in multiple foreach loops, .Count() + iteration, or passed to multiple methods.
baseContent.exampleComment: This IEnumerable from the database query is enumerated twice: once for .Count() and once for the foreach. Materialize with .ToList() first.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Materialize IEnumerable before using it more than once.

items.text: Are N+1 query patterns avoided with eager loading or projections?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Accessing navigation properties in a loop triggers a separate query for each entity. Use .Include() for eager loading or .Select() to project only needed fields.
baseContent.whyItMatters: An N+1 pattern on 1000 entities sends 1001 queries. This is the most common performance killer in EF Core applications.
baseContent.howToVerify: Enable EF Core query logging. Look for loops that access navigation properties without .Include() in the original query.
baseContent.exampleComment: Accessing order.Customer inside the loop triggers a separate query per order. Add .Include(o => o.Customer) to the original query.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
var orders = await _context.Orders
    .Include(o => o.Customer)
    .ToListAsync();
foreach (var order in orders)
    Console.WriteLine(order.Customer.Name); // 1 query

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always .Include() navigation properties accessed in loops. Check EF query logs.

items.text: Are LINQ queries evaluated server-side, not client-side?

baseContent.whatItMeans: EF Core throws on client-side evaluation by default, but some operations silently pull all data to memory. Ensure .Where() and .OrderBy() translate to SQL.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Client-side evaluation fetches all rows then filters in memory. On large tables this means transferring millions of rows instead of a targeted query.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for custom C# methods in .Where() clauses that cannot translate to SQL. Enable EF Core logging to see the generated SQL.
baseContent.exampleComment: FormatName() in the .Where() clause cannot translate to SQL, forcing client evaluation. Move the filter to a SQL-translatable expression.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep LINQ predicates SQL-translatable. Watch EF Core's query log for warnings.

items.text: Are thread-shared collections using concurrent or immutable types?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Dictionary<K,V> and List<T> are not thread-safe. Use ConcurrentDictionary, ImmutableList, or lock for shared state.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Concurrent reads and writes to a Dictionary can corrupt its internal state, causing infinite loops or lost data. These bugs are intermittent and hard to reproduce.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check static or singleton collections that are written to. Are they protected by a lock, or using a concurrent type?
baseContent.exampleComment: This static Dictionary<string, Cache> is written to from multiple request threads. Switch to ConcurrentDictionary to avoid corruption.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Non-concurrent collections + multiple threads = corruption. Always use concurrent types.

items.text: Are DI service lifetimes correct (Scoped, Transient, Singleton)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Injecting a Scoped service into a Singleton creates a captive dependency: the scoped instance is reused across all requests, leaking state.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Captive dependency bugs cause data leaks between requests (e.g., one user seeing another's data via a shared DbContext). ASP.NET Core detects this in Development but not in Production.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that Singleton services do not inject Scoped services. Enable ValidateScopes in the host builder for runtime detection.
baseContent.exampleComment: CacheService is Singleton but injects AppDbContext (Scoped). The DbContext will be reused across requests, causing stale data and threading issues.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
services.AddSingleton<CacheService>();  // Singleton
services.AddScoped<AppDbContext>();       // Scoped

public class CacheService(AppDbContext db) // captive Scoped!
{ }

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never inject Scoped into Singleton. Use IServiceScopeFactory when a Singleton needs scoped services.

items.text: Is the service locator anti-pattern avoided?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Injecting IServiceProvider and resolving services manually hides dependencies and makes testing harder. Prefer constructor injection.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Service locator makes it impossible to see a class's dependencies from its constructor. It also bypasses the DI container's lifetime management.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for IServiceProvider.GetService or GetRequiredService outside of factory methods or middleware. These should usually be constructor-injected dependencies.
baseContent.exampleComment: This class resolves ILogger from IServiceProvider manually. Inject ILogger<T> through the constructor instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Constructor injection over service locator. Reserve IServiceProvider for factories.

items.text: Are IDisposable services properly disposed?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Services that implement IDisposable (DbContext, HttpClient, streams) must be disposed to release unmanaged resources. The DI container handles this for registered services, but manually created instances must be disposed explicitly.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Leaked disposables cause connection pool exhaustion, file handle leaks, and memory pressure. HttpClient is especially problematic: creating new instances per request exhausts sockets.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for new HttpClient() (use IHttpClientFactory instead). Check for manually created disposables that are not in a using block.
baseContent.exampleComment: new HttpClient() per request will exhaust sockets. Use IHttpClientFactory or inject HttpClient via DI.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use IHttpClientFactory. Dispose manually created resources. Let DI handle registered services.

items.text: Are nullable reference types (NRT) enabled and used correctly?

baseContent.whatItMeans: C# nullable reference types make nullability explicit. string is non-null, string? is nullable. The compiler warns on potential null dereferences.
baseContent.whyItMatters: NullReferenceException is the most common runtime error in .NET. NRT catches these at compile time instead of production.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that <Nullable>enable</Nullable> is in the .csproj. Look for ! (null-forgiving operator) usage, which suppresses warnings and defeats the purpose.
baseContent.exampleComment: The ! operator on line 12 suppresses a legitimate null warning. Handle the null case explicitly instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
string name = user.DisplayName!; // suppresses warning
Console.WriteLine(name.Length);

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Enable NRT. Avoid the ! operator. Handle nulls explicitly.

items.text: Are records used for immutable data transfer objects?

baseContent.whatItMeans: C# records provide value equality, immutability, and concise syntax. They are ideal for DTOs, events, and value objects.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Records reduce boilerplate (no manual Equals/GetHashCode), prevent accidental mutation, and make intent clear.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new DTOs and value types. Are they classes with only getters? They would be better as records.
baseContent.exampleComment: This UserDto class has only init properties and no behavior. Convert to a record for built-in equality and immutability.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code: public record UserDto(int Id, string Name, string Email);

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use records for data carriers. Classes for entities with behavior.

items.text: Is pattern matching used instead of type-checking casts?

baseContent.whatItMeans: C# pattern matching (is, switch expressions) replaces verbose if-is-cast chains with cleaner syntax.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Pattern matching is more readable, avoids double evaluation, and enables exhaustiveness checking with switch expressions.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for if (x is Type t) or cast chains that could be simplified with switch expressions.
baseContent.exampleComment: This if-else chain checking types would be clearer as a switch expression with pattern matching.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Prefer switch expressions with patterns for type-based dispatch.

items.text: Is model validation applied to all API inputs?

baseContent.whatItMeans: API endpoints should validate request bodies using data annotations ([Required], [Range], etc.) or FluentValidation. The framework handles 400 responses automatically.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unvalidated input is the root of injection attacks, data corruption, and crashes. Validation at the API boundary catches bad data before it reaches business logic.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check [FromBody] and [FromQuery] parameters. Do they have validation attributes? Is [ApiController] on the controller (enables automatic model validation)?
baseContent.exampleComment: The CreateOrderRequest model has no validation. Add [Required] to Name and [Range(1, 10000)] to Quantity.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Validate all inputs at the API boundary. Trust nothing from the client.

items.text: Is middleware registered in the correct order?

baseContent.whatItMeans: ASP.NET Core middleware runs in the order registered. Authentication must come before Authorization, which must come before endpoint routing.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Middleware in the wrong order can bypass security (auth after endpoints), cause errors (CORS after routing), or silently misbehave.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check Program.cs / Startup.cs. The typical order: ExceptionHandler, HSTS, HttpsRedirection, Static Files, Routing, CORS, Authentication, Authorization, Endpoints.
baseContent.exampleComment: UseAuthorization is called before UseAuthentication. This means authorization runs without a user identity. Swap the order.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Middleware order matters. Authentication before Authorization before Endpoints.

items.text: Are minimal API endpoints following conventions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Minimal APIs should use typed Results (Results.Ok, Results.NotFound), group endpoints with MapGroup, and use TypedResults for OpenAPI metadata.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Consistent endpoint conventions make the API predictable, improve OpenAPI generation, and make testing easier.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that endpoints return TypedResults, use [FromBody]/[FromQuery] correctly, and follow REST naming conventions.
baseContent.exampleComment: This endpoint returns Ok(new {}) for errors. Use Results.Problem or Results.NotFound for proper HTTP semantics.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use TypedResults for OpenAPI. Follow REST conventions.

items.text: Are exceptions handled centrally, not in each controller?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use middleware (UseExceptionHandler) or IExceptionHandler for centralized error handling instead of try-catch in every action method.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Scattered try-catch blocks lead to inconsistent error responses, duplicated logging, and missed exceptions. Central handling ensures consistent error format.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if controllers have try-catch blocks. These usually belong in middleware. Exceptions should bubble up and be caught centrally.
baseContent.exampleComment: This try-catch in the controller duplicates the error handling in 5 other controllers. Move to UseExceptionHandler middleware.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Handle exceptions in middleware, not in controllers. Let exceptions bubble.

items.text: Are EF Core migrations safe for production rollout?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Migrations should be backwards-compatible, avoid data loss, and not lock tables for extended periods. Column renames, type changes, and NOT NULL additions require care.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A migration that locks a table for minutes causes downtime. A migration that drops a column loses data. These are not reversible with a rollback.
baseContent.howToVerify: Review the generated migration SQL. Check for ALTER TABLE on large tables, DROP COLUMN, and data-type changes. Test on a production-sized dataset.
baseContent.exampleComment: This migration adds a NOT NULL column without a default value. Existing rows will fail. Add a default value or make it nullable first.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Review generated SQL, not just the C# migration. Test on production-sized data.

items.text: Is change tracking disabled for read-only queries?

baseContent.whatItMeans: EF Core tracks all queried entities by default, which adds memory and CPU overhead. For read-only queries, use .AsNoTracking().
baseContent.whyItMatters: Tracking 10,000 entities for a read-only list page wastes memory and slows down DetectChanges. AsNoTracking can be 2-5x faster for read-heavy workloads.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check queries that do not modify data. Are they using .AsNoTracking()? Consider UseQueryTrackingBehavior(NoTracking) as the default.
baseContent.exampleComment: This list endpoint fetches 500 orders for display only. Add .AsNoTracking() to avoid unnecessary change tracking overhead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Read-only queries should use AsNoTracking(). Consider making it the default.

items.text: Is DbContext scoped per request, not singleton or static?

baseContent.whatItMeans: DbContext is not thread-safe. It must be scoped per request in web apps. Never store it in a static field or register it as singleton.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A shared DbContext causes data corruption, stale data, and concurrency exceptions. Each request should get its own instance.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check DI registration. AddDbContext defaults to Scoped, which is correct. Look for static DbContext fields or manual instantiation.
baseContent.exampleComment: This static DbContext field is shared across all requests. Remove it and inject DbContext through the constructor.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: DbContext is per-request. Never share it across threads.

items.text: Are raw SQL queries parameterized?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When using FromSqlRaw or ExecuteSqlRaw, parameters must use {0} placeholders or SqlParameter objects, never string interpolation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: SQL injection remains the most dangerous web vulnerability. EF's FromSqlInterpolated is safe, but FromSqlRaw with concatenation is not.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for FromSqlRaw and ExecuteSqlRaw. Ensure they use parameterized placeholders. Prefer FromSqlInterpolated which handles parameters automatically.
baseContent.exampleComment: This FromSqlRaw uses string concatenation with user input. Switch to FromSqlInterpolated to prevent SQL injection.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use FromSqlInterpolated, not FromSqlRaw with concatenation. EF handles parameterization.

items.text: Are secrets stored in configuration, not in source code?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Connection strings, API keys, and passwords should come from environment variables, Azure Key Vault, or user-secrets, never hardcoded.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Secrets in source code end up in version control. Even private repos can be compromised. Secrets should be managed externally and rotated regularly.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for hardcoded strings that look like passwords, API keys, or connection strings. Check appsettings.json for production secrets.
baseContent.exampleComment: The connection string in appsettings.json contains a password. Use environment variables or user-secrets for local dev, Key Vault for production.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: No secrets in code or config files. Use environment variables, user-secrets, or a vault.

items.text: Are endpoints protected with appropriate authorization?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every endpoint that modifies data or returns sensitive information should have [Authorize] or policy-based authorization.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing authorization means any authenticated (or unauthenticated) user can access the endpoint. This is a common source of privilege escalation.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new endpoints for [Authorize], [AllowAnonymous], or RequireAuthorization(). Ensure role/policy checks match the endpoint's sensitivity.
baseContent.exampleComment: This admin endpoint has no [Authorize] attribute. Any user can call it. Add [Authorize(Roles = "Admin")] or a policy.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Default to authorized. Explicitly mark public endpoints with [AllowAnonymous].

items.text: Do tests follow the Arrange-Act-Assert pattern?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each test should have three clear phases: set up data (Arrange), execute the code under test (Act), and verify the outcome (Assert).
baseContent.whyItMatters: AAA makes tests readable and maintainable. Tests without clear structure are hard to debug when they fail.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that each test method has a clear separation between setup, execution, and assertions. Avoid multiple acts in one test.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test mixes setup and assertions. Restructure with clear Arrange, Act, Assert sections.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: One Act per test. Arrange, Act, Assert. Every time.

items.text: Are integration tests using WebApplicationFactory?

baseContent.whatItMeans: WebApplicationFactory<Program> creates an in-memory test server. It is the standard way to write integration tests for ASP.NET Core without deploying.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Integration tests catch issues that unit tests miss: middleware, DI configuration, routing, and serialization. WebApplicationFactory makes them easy to write.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that API tests use WebApplicationFactory, not manual HttpClient setup against a running server.
baseContent.exampleComment: These tests spin up a real server on localhost. Use WebApplicationFactory for faster, more reliable integration tests.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: WebApplicationFactory for integration tests. It boots the full pipeline in-process.

items.text: Are mocks used only at boundaries, not for internal logic?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Mock external dependencies (databases, APIs, file systems) but not internal classes. Over-mocking creates brittle tests that break on refactoring.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Tests that mock every dependency test the mocking setup, not the code. They give false confidence and slow down refactoring.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for tests with 5+ mock setups. Usually that means the class has too many dependencies or the tests are over-specified.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test mocks 6 internal services. Consider testing with real implementations and only mocking the external API calls.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Mock at boundaries (DB, API, filesystem). Use real objects for internal logic.
